did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes Swin-RSCBNet, a transformer-based deep learning architecture for skin lesion segmentation to aid in melanoma detection. It utilizes a Swin Transformer backbone and custom modules (CBAM, RFB, SE) to segment skin lesions in dermoscopic images from the PH2 and ISIC 2018 datasets, achieving Dice scores of 96.69% and 91.75% respectively.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a novel deep learning architecture (Swin-RSCBNet) for skin cancer segmentation. It includes a methodology section detailing the model design, an experiments section with ablation studies and comparisons on the PH2 and ISIC 2018 datasets, and reports new quantitative results (Dice scores, IoU). Although published in a conference proceedings (ICCKE), it contains original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Swin-RSCBNet: A Transformer-based Network for Skin Cancer Segmentation with Multi-scale and Attention Modules
journal: 2025 15th International Conference on Computer and Knowledge Engineering (ICCKE)
brief_summary: This study proposes Swin-RSCBNet, a lightweight transformer-based architecture for skin lesion segmentation to aid in early melanoma detection. The model utilizes a Swin Transformer encoder and a novel decoder block integrating Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM), Receptive Field Block (RFB), and Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) modules. It was evaluated on the PH2 and ISIC 2018 dermoscopic image datasets using Dice Coefficient (DC) and Intersection over Union (IoU) metrics. The proposed method achieved superior performance with a DC of 96.69% and IoU of 93.59% on PH2, and a DC of 91.75% and IoU of 84.75% on ISIC 2018. These results demonstrate that Swin-RSCBNet outperforms existing state-of-the-art models while maintaining a significantly lower parameter count of 33.80 million.

lesion_types_studied.details: The paper focuses on the segmentation of skin lesions in dermoscopic images from the PH2 and ISIC 2018 datasets. The task is described as segmenting 'skin lesion anomalies' and 'suspicious lesions' to aid in early detection.

Melanoma is identified as the primary clinical entity of interest for early detection. The abstract states the model aids in 'melanoma detection' and identifies regions 'potentially indicative of melanoma'. The introduction notes melanoma is 'one of the most dangerous forms of skin tumors'. The ISIC 2018 dataset is known to contain melanoma and other classes, but the paper treats the segmentation task generally on the provided masks without explicitly listing all histological classes used in the dataset split.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion masks (binary segmentation of lesion anomalies from dermoscopic images)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly focuses on 'skin lesion segmentation' to aid in melanoma detection. The model predicts pixel-wise masks for skin lesions using the PH2 and ISIC 2018 datasets. Performance is evaluated using Dice Coefficient (DC) and Intersection over Union (IoU) metrics, which are standard for segmentation tasks. The loss function combines binary cross-entropy (BCE) and IoU.

datasets_used.name: PH2
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dermoscopic image database for research and benchmarking, consisting of dermoscopic images with associated segmentation masks.

use.training: Used for training the Swin-RSCBNet model (176 images) and validation (24 images).
use.testing: Used for final evaluation/testing of the model's segmentation performance.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset containing dermoscopic images with corresponding segmentation masks, used to evaluate the network's capability on a larger dataset.

use.training: Used for training the Swin-RSCBNet model (2334 images) and validation (260 images).
use.testing: Used for final evaluation/testing of the model's segmentation performance.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using point estimates of Dice Coefficient and IoU metrics without statistical significance analysis.

PH2.notes: Swin-RSCBNet achieved the highest DC and IoU scores on PH2. The model uses 33.80M parameters, which is significantly lower than competing methods like TransUNet (109.07M) and Swin-UNet (97.70M).

ISIC 2018.notes: Swin-RSCBNet achieved the highest DC (91.75%) and IoU (84.75%) on ISIC 2018. The training loss and validation loss decreased gradually over epochs, indicating no overfitting.

architecture_used.pretraining: Swin Transformer Tiny model pre-trained on ImageNet

architecture_details.decoder: Custom RSCB block containing Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM), Receptive Field Block (RFB), and Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) modules
architecture_details.cbam_implementation: Modified CBAM using only adaptive average pooling in the channel attention mechanism, omitting the max-pooling pathway
architecture_details.rfb_implementation: Multi-branch module with one identity branch and three branches using different dilation rates (d=1, 3, 9) with 7x7 convolutions
architecture_details.se_implementation: Squeeze-and-Excitation block for channel-wise feature recalibration

training_details.loss_function: Combined Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE) and Intersection over Union (IoU)
training_details.hardware: RTX 3070 GPU, Intel Core i3-8100 CPU, 32 GB DDR5 RAM

classification_or_output_head.details: Pixel-wise binary segmentation of skin lesion anomalies

preprocessing_techniques: Pixel value normalization by dividing by 255
conclusions: The proposed Swin-RSCBNet architecture, which integrates a Swin Transformer backbone with a custom RSCB block (combining CBAM, RFB, and SE modules), achieves superior skin lesion segmentation accuracy compared to existing state-of-the-art models.
Swin-RSCBNet achieved a Dice Coefficient of 96.69% and IoU of 93.59% on the PH2 dataset, and a Dice Coefficient of 91.75% and IoU of 84.75% on the ISIC 2018 dataset.
The model is lightweight, requiring only 33.80 million parameters, which is significantly lower than competing methods such as TransUNet (109.07M) and Swin-UNet (97.70M).

The integration of multi-scale contextual information via the Receptive Field Block (RFB) and attention mechanisms (CBAM and SE) significantly enhances the transformer's segmentation capability by improving focus on salient features and channel-wise recalibration.
The model demonstrates robustness in handling lesions with varying sizes and blurry boundaries, effectively segmenting both small and large skin lesions.
The training process showed no signs of overfitting, as indicated by the gradual decrease in both training and validation loss over epochs.

The simplified CBAM implementation, which uses only adaptive average pooling in the channel attention mechanism, reduces computational complexity while preserving attention quality, making the model suitable for resource-constrained applications.
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation, relying solely on two public datasets (PH2 and ISIC 2018) without testing on independent, unseen clinical cohorts.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the superiority of Swin-RSCBNet over competing models like CAT-UNet.
The PH2 dataset is small (200 images total), which may limit the generalizability of the high performance metrics (96.69% DC) reported for this specific dataset.
The paper does not report data leakage risks or detailed cross-validation protocols beyond the stated train/validation splits (176/24 for PH2; 2334/260 for ISIC 2018).
The model was trained on resized 224x224 images, which may distort aspect ratios and affect segmentation accuracy for lesions with non-standard shapes.
No data augmentation techniques were employed, which is a limitation for a study claiming robustness on limited data (PH2).
The claim of 'no overfitting' is based solely on the observation of decreasing training and validation loss curves, without quantitative metrics like variance or statistical tests.

The hardware used (Intel Core i3-8100 CPU, RTX 3070 GPU) is consumer-grade, which may not reflect performance in clinical deployment environments requiring higher throughput or different infrastructure.
The paper does not address potential bias in the ISIC 2018 dataset regarding skin tone diversity or demographic representation, which is critical for dermatological AI.
The simplified CBAM implementation omits the max-pooling pathway, which is a design choice that reduces complexity but may impact feature extraction capabilities compared to the original CBAM.
